title: fire_power_lines
description: A downed power line ignites a wildfire during high winds, complicating suppression with energized line hazards.

timeline.title: Power Line Down — Fire Ignites
timeline.description: Downed transmission line ignites dry grass. Utility company notified immediately.

timeline.title: Energized Line Hazard Confirmed
timeline.description: Utility reports line is still energized. No crew within 30 feet until confirmed de-energized.

timeline.title: De-energization Delayed
timeline.description: Utility de-energization will take 45 minutes. Fire spreading. Indirect tactics required.

timeline.title: Evacuation of Adjacent Properties
timeline.description: Homes within the line hazard zone being evacuated. No entry until utility confirms safe.

timeline.title: Line Confirmed De-energized
timeline.description: Utility confirms line is de-energized and grounded. Ground crew access now permitted.

timeline.title: Direct Suppression Begins
timeline.description: Ground crews engage directly now that hazard is cleared. Air support continues.

node-1.title: Power Line Ignition
node-1.situation: High winds have toppled a transmission line which is sparking and has ignited dry grass nearby. The fire is spreading. Crews are on scene but have been warned the line is still energized.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Establish a 100-foot exclusion zone around the line, contact the utility company for emergency de-energization, and begin indirect suppression tactics.

options.label: B
options.text: Have crews attack the fire at its base near the downed line since it is the quickest suppression option.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait for utility company confirmation before taking any action at the scene.

node-2.title: De-energization Delay
node-2.situation: The utility company reports it will take 45 minutes to de-energize the line. During this time, the fire continues to spread. Air tankers are available but cannot safely drop near the line.
node-2.question: How do you use air resources while the line remains energized?

options.label: A
options.text: Direct air tankers to drop retardant ahead of the fire's direction of spread, away from the line hazard zone.

options.label: B
options.text: Have air tankers attempt drops near the line since retardant is non-conductive.

node-3.title: Structure Threatened in Hazard Zone
node-3.situation: A residential structure is now at risk within the line hazard zone. The homeowner is reportedly still inside. Sending crews in is not authorized until the line is de-energized.
node-3.question: How do you handle the occupant in the hazard zone?

options.label: A
options.text: Attempt to communicate with the occupant via phone or loudspeaker and guide them to safely exit the hazard zone on foot away from the line.

options.label: B
options.text: Send a fire crew into the hazard zone to rescue the occupant immediately.

node-4.title: Line De-energized — Crew Deployment
node-4.situation: The utility company confirms the line is de-energized and grounded. Crews can now enter the former hazard zone for direct attack. The fire has grown during the delay.
node-4.question: How do you prioritize direct attack deployment?

options.label: A
options.text: Deploy ground crews to structure defense and direct fire attack simultaneously, coordinating with air operations.

options.label: B
options.text: Focus all ground crews on structure defense and leave fire attack to air resources only.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The power line hazard was managed safely. The structure was protected and the occupant evacuated without injury. The fire is now contained with ground and air resources working in coordination.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
